pages.raw_text:
The amalgamated sheet metal
workers would not give out any of the
proceedings Of their meeting. How
ever, the president of the union stated
this morning that the organization
would be found in line despite any
report to the contrary. Other men,
bers Of the union said that they never
fail to recognize the action Of the
trades council.


The shinsle weavers and the laun-
dry workers unions also voted to a
man to walk out, so that out Of the
five organizations that assembled last
night there was not one dissenting
voice.


e. c. Potts, a member Of the trades
council, in speaking of the position
that he took in the council when the
strike question came up, said this
morning:


Il don't want you to publish my
name, although I am not particular
whether you do or not. but wish to
state that. although I voted against
the measure in the council. am now
strongly in favor of 1t. Whatever our
views may be previous to a decision
of the central body, once 1t takes q
stand we always chime right in. The
gas workers can bank on me now"
About the largest and most innuen-
tial union in the city Is the building
laborers. An officer Of that organiza-
tion said today, "All want to say
Is. that the building laborers will GER
tainly vote in favor of the recon-
mendation of the trades council"
Prominent business men expressed
themselves today upon the best means
| in their estimation Of warding on the
trouble. No one doubts that a gen.
eral. strike will paralyxe the business
interests of the city and that 1t might
take long time to get over the evil
effects that would inevitably ensue.
The general opinion Is that an arbi-
| tration committee consisting Of 10
leading business men and 10 repre-
sentatives Of labor be appointed to
settle the matter with the gas COM
pany. The following names have been
